CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its two main contributions (transformation-based predictor and a classifier-based evaluation) early in the Introduction. Notation for patches and affine transforms is defined and mostly consistent, and the text describes experiments and evaluation clearly. Presentation issues: several small inconsistencies (e.g. listed number of convolutional layers vs. the number of feature‑map counts in the UCF-101 paragraph), some awkward phrasing and minor grammatical slips, and duplicated/garbled paragraphs at the end of the provided text which hinder readability. Figures and tables are frequently referenced and seem appropriate, but are not present in the excerpt which makes it hard to judge their effectiveness here.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a distinct perspective—modeling and predicting local affine transformations (per overlapping patch) instead of raw pixels (Sec.1 and 2.1)—which is clearly different from prior pixel-wise regression, optical-flow baselines, and adversarial pixel generators (Related Work). Its evaluation protocol (feed generated frames to a classifier trained on ground-truth, Sec.2.4) is also a novel and practical metric extension of prior attribute-recovery ideas. The core components (CNNs, affine warps, multi-step unrolling) are mostly existing techniques, so the contribution is a notable and well-differentiated innovation rather than a radically new algorithmic paradigm.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims (modeling local affine transforms and evaluating generated video via a pretrained classifier) and supports them with experiments on Moving MNIST and UCF-101, including comparisons to sensible baselines (copy last frame, optical-flow extrapolation, Mathieu et al.). There are no formal mathematical derivations required and the algorithmic description is coherent; the affine-extractor and CNN predictor are plausible. Experimental methodology is reasonable (use of C3D classifier, ablations on patch size/frames), but important details and numeric results/tables are omitted here, statistical significance is not reported, and one baseline (LSTM) is absent for scale reasons — these are minor but relevant limitations. Overall the conclusions follow from the presented experiments, though some claims (e.g. ‘‘negligible loss’’ from affine reconstruction, FLOP/parameter comparisons) would benefit from more complete quantitative evidence and reporting.
